title: About
text:
Started as a hack-day project at the Sofware Sustainability Institute Collaboration Workshop 2026.
This resource was created as a welcoming entry to coding and research software engineering where everyone is welcome.
We want to empower community, collaboration, and networking.

title: Sophie Wilson
text:
Sophie Wilson (born June 1957) is a British computer scientist best known for her central role in creating the BBC Micro computer, and designing the original ARM processor architecture.
While working at Acorn Computers in the late 1970s and 1980s, she helped to develop BBC BASIC. Her work on ARM began as a project to build a fast, efficient processor for Acorn’s computers, but ARM later became the foundation for billions of devices, especially smartphones, tablets, embedded systems, and low-power computers.
She has received major recognition for her contributions, including being elected a Fellow of the Royal Society. Her career helped shape both personal computing education and modern mobile computing.
Wilson’s success challenges the idea that LGBTQ+ people, and especially trans people, are marginal to science and engineering. Wilson’s career shows that a trans woman was central to one of the most important technological developments of the late twentieth century.


 





Sophie Wilson


Photo credit, available under CC BY 2.0

title: Lynn Ann Conway
text:
Lynn Ann Conway was an American computer scientist and electrical engineer (1938–2024) whose technical career included early work at IBM, leadership at Xerox PARC and the University of Michigan. She is highly recognised for her work on generalized dynamic instruction handling, an important precursor to out-of-order execution. With Carver Mead she coauthored “Introduction to VLSI Systems” and helped create the “Mead–Conway Very-Large-Scale-Integration (VLSI) methodology” that democratized chip design and shaped modern microelectronics.
Conway’s faced immense workplace discrimination after revealing her intent to gender transition, which resulted to her being dismissed from IBM in 1968. This had effects on how and when she could claim credit for her work, leading to her having to live much of her professional life quietly under a different identity before coming out publicly in 1999 and documenting her story and resources for others. This experience shaped her later advocacy for transgender people and increased visibility for trans professionals in STEM.
Her technical and social legacy persists even after her death in 2024: The Mead–Conway methods underpin electronic design automation and VLSI education worldwide. Her public advocacy, mentorship resources and the later formal apology from IBM have contributed to broader recognition of transgender scientists and institutional reckonings with past discrimination. Many engineers and educators trace current chip-design practices and more inclusive cultures in computing back to her influence.


 





Lynn Conway


Photo credit, available under public domain

title: Alan Turing
text:
Considered in many ways to be the father of modern computing, Turing was a pioneering mathematician and computer scientist who introduced the ‘Turing machine’, formalising the concepts of algorithms and computation. His work as part of the code breaking team at Bletchley Park played a crucial role in deciphering German communications and saving countless allied lives.
In 1952, Turing was charged with ‘gross indecency’ under Section 11 of the Criminal Law Amendment Act 1885, and forced to choose between imprisonment and probation under the condition that he undergo ‘chemical castration’. He chose probation, was stripped of his security clearance, and barred from working with GCHQ.
Turing died of cyanide poisoning in 1954, and his death was deemed a suicide. Though this theory has come under considerable doubt, there is no question that Turing was mistreated by the very state he had admirably served. In recognition of this, the UK government passed the ‘Alan Turing law’ in 2017 to retrospectively pardon those who had been persecuted under homophobic laws.


 





Alan Turing


Photo credit, available under public domain

title: Communities
text: This page contains some brief information about some of the LGBTQIA+ communities in tech. These are a living lists and definitely not complete, so if you know of any others that should be added, please get in touch!

title: Communities
text:
Community Spaces



Group
Website
Description




LGBTQ in Technology
https://lgbtq.technology
The LGBTQ in Technology Slack is a space for LGBTQ people in technology to chat and support each other.


Queer Code
https://queer-code.org
We are a community of queer coders. We build tech together, share our ideas at local meetups, and provide support for queer people working in software development.


QueerJS
https://queerjs.com
QueerJS is a meetup series where everyone is encouraged to attend and support the speakers and the idea.


rainbowR
https://rainbowr.org
Our mission is to connect, support and promote LGBTQ+ people in the R community and to spread awareness of LGBTQ+ issues through data-driven activism

title: Communities
section: Advocacy and Visibility and More Formal Organisations
text:
Advocacy and Visibility and More Formal Organisations



Group
Website
Description




Pride in Stem
https://prideinstem.org
Pride in STEM is a charity run by an independent group of LGBTQIA+ scientists & engineers from around the world. Proud of who we are and what we do. We aim to showcase and support all LGBTQIA+ people in STEM fields.


TransTech
https://transtechsocial.org
TransTech is an incubator for LGBTQ Talent with a focus on economically empowering the T, transgender people, in our community. TransTech is a co-working, co-learning community dedicated to empowering trans, lesbian, gay, bisexual, queer people and allies with practical, career-ready skills.

title: Communities
text:
Academics Groups



Group
Website
Description




500 Queer Scientists
https://500queerscientists.com
A visibility campaign for LGBTQ+ people and their allies working in STEM and STEM-supporting jobs


ALDER Network
https://www.britishecologicalsociety.org/content/join-the-alder-network
A network for the LGBTQIA+ ecologist community to interact, share and support each other.


NYU Queer Data Lab
https://wp.nyu.edu/queer_data_lab
Queering and Elevating Empirical Research through Data


Out to Innovate
https://www.outtoinnovate.org/home
Out to Innovate is a professional society and global community of LGBTQ+ students and professionals in science, technology, engineering, and mathematics.


Queer, Equality and Diversity (QED) Network for Maths
https://sites.google.com/view/qednetwork/home
The QED network is a community-led effort by queer mathematical staff (including PhD students) to connect and support LGBTQIA+ members of the UK mathematics community.


Spectra
https://www.lgbtmath.org
The Association for LGBT Mathematicians
